agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order cancellation feature for an order management system. Three stakeholders (Product, Finance, Operations) have provided requirements that govern when an order may be cancelled, how invoicing works, and when invoicing occurs in the order lifecycle.","bounded_context":"Order Management / Order Lifecycle","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Order","definition":"A customer's request to purchase goods, tracked through a lifecycle of states from placement to shipment."},{"term":"Cancellation","definition":"The act of voiding an order so it will not be fulfilled or shipped."},{"term":"Invoice","definition":"A financial document generated against an order that locks in pricing and represents a commitment to collect payment."},{"term":"Shipment","definition":"The physical dispatch of goods to the customer, representing a point after which cancellation is no longer requested."},{"term":"Order Placement","definition":"The initial act of creating and confirming an order in the system."},{"term":"Invoicing","definition":"The irreversible process of generating an invoice against an order, per Finance's constraint."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"An order may be cancelled at any time before it ships.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a business policy from Product defining the cancellation window. A VP could adjust this window (e.g., restrict to 30 minutes after placement).","testable_assertion":"GIVEN an order in any pre-shipment state, WHEN a cancellation is requested, THEN the order transitions to Cancelled."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"An invoiced order cannot be cancelled. Invoicing is irreversible.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Finance treats invoicing as an irreversible financial commitment. Allowing cancellation of invoiced orders would corrupt the financial ledger and violate accounting integrity.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN an order that has been invoiced, WHEN a cancellation is requested, THEN the system rejects the cancellation."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Orders are invoiced at the time of placement to lock in pricing.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is an operational policy choice about when to invoice. Operations could decide to defer invoicing to a later stage (e.g., at pick/pack) without corrupting system state.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN a new order is placed, WHEN placement is confirmed, THEN an invoice is immediately generated for that order."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"An order exists that has not yet shipped and has not been invoiced","when":"A cancellation request is submitted for that order","then":"The order is cancelled successfully"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"An order has been invoiced","when":"A cancellation request is submitted","then":"The system rejects the cancellation with an explanation that invoiced orders cannot be cancelled"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"An order is placed","when":"The placement is confirmed by the system","then":"An invoice is generated immediately for that order"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"An order has been placed (and therefore invoiced per BR-3)","when":"A cancellation request is submitted before shipment","then":"CONFLICT — BR-1 says allow cancellation; BR-2 says reject because the order is invoiced. Behavior is undefined until the contradiction is resolved."}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Order placed but invoice generation fails or is delayed due to a system error","expected_behavior":"Ambiguous — if the order exists without an invoice, BR-1 allows cancellation, but Operations expects all placed orders to be invoiced. Must define whether a placed-but-uninvoiced order is a valid state."},{"scenario":"Partial shipment — some items shipped, others not","expected_behavior":"Undefined — BR-1 says 'before it ships' but does not clarify partial shipment scenarios. Need to clarify whether cancellation applies to unshipped line items."},{"scenario":"Customer requests cancellation at the exact moment of placement/invoicing","expected_behavior":"Race condition — depending on whether invoicing completes before the cancellation request is processed, the outcome differs. Need to define ordering guarantees."},{"scenario":"Credit memo or refund issued against an invoice","expected_behavior":"Finance says invoicing is irreversible, but the business may need a credit memo workflow as a workaround for effective cancellation. This is a common pattern in logistics but is not mentioned in the requirements."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"If the contradiction is resolved by preventing all cancellations (Finance wins), customer satisfaction drops and refund/chargeback costs may rise. If resolved by allowing cancellations of invoiced orders, financial ledger integrity is at risk and audit/compliance exposure increases.","operational":"Operations invoices at placement to lock pricing. Any resolution that defers invoicing changes their pricing-lock workflow. Any resolution that introduces credit memos adds operational complexity.","user":"Customers expect to cancel orders before shipment. If the system silently prevents cancellation of all placed orders, it will appear broken or hostile to end users.","risk":"HIGH — shipping an implementation without resolving this contradiction guarantees one stakeholder's invariant is violated. If Finance's rule is violated, audit and compliance risk. If Product's rule is violated, customer experience and revenue risk.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Cannot cancel any order after placement if Finance's rule is enforced literally, despite PM's promise of pre-shipment cancellation."},{"group":"Finance / Accounting","impact":"If cancellation of invoiced orders is allowed, ledger integrity is compromised; they may need a credit memo or reversal process."},{"group":"Operations","impact":"If invoicing is deferred to allow a cancellation window, their pricing-lock mechanism breaks and they face price volatility risk."},{"group":"Product / Customer Support","impact":"Will receive escalations from customers unable to cancel orders, increasing support volume and reducing NPS."}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"Three explicit stakeholder requirements directly contradict each other when composed. BR-1 + BR-3 implies all placed orders are immediately invoiced, and BR-2 says invoiced orders cannot be cancelled — making BR-1 impossible to satisfy for any placed order. The spec is internally inconsistent and no implementation can satisfy all three rules simultaneously. Confidence is very low because the core feature (cancellation) is blocked by this unresolved conflict.","high_confidence_areas":["Each individual business rule is clearly stated and unambiguous in isolation","The contradiction is definitive and not a matter of interpretation","Invoice irreversibility is a genuine financial integrity constraint"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether Operations would accept deferred invoicing to open a cancellation window","Whether Finance would accept a credit memo pattern as a workaround for cancellation","Whether 'cancelled' could mean something different from 'voided' in the financial context"],"assumptions":["All three stakeholder statements represent current, authoritative policy","There is no existing credit memo or invoice reversal process that would reconcile these rules","Invoicing and order placement are synchronous operations"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"BR-1 (cancel before shipment) is impossible to satisfy when combined with BR-3 (invoice at placement) and BR-2 (invoiced orders cannot be cancelled). Every placed order is immediately invoiced, and no invoiced order can be cancelled — therefore no order can ever be cancelled, directly contradicting the PM's requirement. The three stakeholders have provided mutually exclusive constraints.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Management","Finance","Operations","Customers","Customer Support"],"options":["A: Finance wins — no cancellations allowed post-placement. PM must retract the cancellation feature or redefine it as a 'return' or 'refund request' post-shipment only.","B: Product wins — introduce a credit memo / invoice reversal process so that invoiced orders CAN be effectively cancelled pre-shipment. Finance must accept that 'irreversible' means the original invoice persists but is offset by a credit note.","C: Operations compromises — defer invoicing to a later stage (e.g., pick/pack or shipment) to create a cancellation window between placement and invoicing. Operations must find another mechanism to lock pricing.","D: Hybrid — introduce an order state between Placed and Invoiced (e.g., 'Confirmed') with a short cancellation window, then invoice after the window closes. Balances all three concerns but adds lifecycle complexity."],"recommendation":"Option B (credit memo pattern) is the industry-standard resolution in logistics and e-commerce. The invoice remains irreversible as a ledger entry, satisfying Finance's audit requirements, while a credit memo effectively reverses the financial impact, enabling cancellation. This preserves Operations' pricing lock and Product's cancellation promise. Recommend convening all three stakeholders to confirm."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"The three requirements as stated cannot all be implemented simultaneously. Any implementation will violate at least one stakeholder's explicit constraint.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1 (cancellation before shipment) is rendered impossible by the combination of BR-2 (no cancellation of invoiced orders) and BR-3 (invoice at placement)."}]}
agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order cancellation feature for an order management system. Three stakeholders (Product, Finance, Operations) have provided requirements that govern when an order may be cancelled, how invoicing works, and when invoicing occurs in the order lifecycle.","bounded_context":"Order Management / Order Lifecycle","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Order","definition":"A customer's request to purchase goods, tracked through a lifecycle of states from placement to ship

content.thinking:
Let me analyze these three requirements:

1. PM: "An order can be cancelled at any time before it ships."
2. Finance: "Orders that have been invoiced cannot be cancelled — invoicing is irreversible."
3. Operations: "We invoice orders at the time of placement to lock in pricing."

This is a clear contradiction:
- If orders are invoiced at placement (Ops), and invoiced orders can't be cancelled (Finance), then orders can NEVER be cancelled.
- But PM says orders can be cancelled any time before shipping.

These are explicit, conflicting requirements from different stakeholders. This is a `contradiction`.

Confidence is very low because the core feature (cancellation) is blocked by this unresolved conflict.","high_confidence_areas":["Each individual business rule is clearly stated and unambiguous in isolation","The contradiction is definitive and not a matter of interpretation","Invoice irreversibility is a genuine financial integrity constraint"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether Operations would accept deferred invoicing to open a cancellation window","Whether Finance would accept a credit memo pattern as a workaround for cancellation","Whether 'cancelled' could mean something different from 'voided' in the financial context"],"assumptions":["All three stakeholder statements represent current, authoritative policy","There is no existing credit memo or invoice reversal process that would reconcile these rules","Invoicing and order placement are synchronous operations"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"BR-1 (cancel before shipment) is impossible to satisfy when combined with BR-3 (invoice at placement) and BR-2 (invoiced orders cannot be cancelled). Every placed order is immediately invoiced, and no invoiced order can be cancelled — therefore no order can ever be cancelled, directly contradicting the PM's requirement. The three stakeholders have provided mutually exclusive constraints.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Management","Finance","Operations","Customers","Customer Support"],"options":["A: Finance wins — no cancellations allowed post-placement. PM must retract the cancellation feature or redefine it as a 'return' or 'refund request' post-shipment only.","B: Product wins — introduce a credit memo / invoice reversal process so that invoiced orders CAN be effectively cancelled pre-shipment. Finance must accept that 'irreversible' means the original invoice persists but is offset by a credit note.","C: Operations compromises — defer invoicing to a later stage (e.g., pick/pack or shipment) to create a cancellation window between placement and invoicing. Operations must find another mechanism to lock pricing.","D: Hybrid — introduce an order state between Placed and Invoiced (e.g., 'Confirmed') with a short cancellation window, then invoice after the window closes. Balances all three concerns but adds lifecycle complexity."],"recommendation":"Option B (credit memo pattern) is the industry-standard resolution in logistics and e-commerce. The invoice remains irreversible as a ledger entry, satisfying Finance's audit requirements, while a credit memo effectively reverses the financial impact, enabling cancellation. This preserves Operations' pricing lock and Product's cancellation promise. Recommend convening all three stakeholders to confirm."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"The three requirements as stated cannot all be implemented simultaneously. Any implementation will violate at least one stakeholder's explicit constraint.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1 (cancellation before shipment) is rendered impossible by the combination of BR-2 (no cancellation of invoiced orders) and BR-3 (invoice at placement)."}]}
